skill.name: ccxt
skill.description: CCXT documentation assistant for building cryptocurrency exchange integrations, market data tools, order workflows, and WebSocket trading applications.
skill.summary: Use CCXT docs to integrate crypto exchange APIs across JavaScript, Python, and PHP.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: The static scanner produced many high-risk alerts, but sampled evidence shows most are false positives from CCXT documentation, Markdown backticks, cryptocurrency terminology, and API examples. The real risk is operational: this skill can help users create exchange integrations, use private API credentials, execute CLI trading calls without confirmation, and route requests through proxies. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Automated Trading Commands Can Move Funds
medium_findings.description: The CLI documentation says commands execute without confirmation and private API calls use credentials. This is legitimate CCXT behavior, but generated guidance can lead to real trades, cancellations, or balance access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is semantically confirmed by documentation warning that the CLI executes without confirmation and by private credential setup guidance. It is not malicious code, so the confidence is high but not critical.

medium_findings.title: Credential Exposure Risk During Debugging
medium_findings.description: The FAQ asks users to provide verbose request and response logs plus runnable code when reporting issues. It also warns not to post API keys or secrets, so the guidance is useful but can expose credentials if users ignore redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly requests verbose logs and reproduction code, while also explicitly warning users to keep secrets safe. This supports a real but mitigated disclosure risk.

medium_findings.title: Proxy and CDN Guidance Can Route Exchange Traffic Through Third Parties
medium_findings.description: The documentation mentions browser CDN bundles and proxy URL prepending for API requests. These are legitimate CCXT features, but copied examples may route trading traffic or metadata through untrusted infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly discuss live CDN use and prepending proxy URLs to API requests. The risk depends on how users apply the guidance, so confidence is moderate-high.

low_findings.title: Network URL Findings Are Expected Documentation
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to CCXT documentation, mirrors, CDN references, or example exchange endpoints. They do not show unauthorized network activity by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are documentation links or explanatory API examples. No evidence found that the skill executes these URLs on its own.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-volume weak-cryptography alerts are caused by cryptocurrency, private/public API, signing, and substring matches in prose. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines are normal CCXT documentation and cryptocurrency domain wording. Because only representative samples were reviewed, confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Backtick Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: External-command alerts mostly match Markdown file names, fenced diagrams, or inline examples. No evidence found that the skill runs shell commands during activation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation index entries, fenced text diagrams, or generated quick-reference prose. They are not executable command dispatch code.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Alert Is Method Notation
low_findings.description: The filesystem alert at the sampled manual line is dot-prefixed CCXT method notation, such as private endpoint method names. It is not reading a hidden file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line describes generated exchange method names and API endpoint naming. There is no filesystem read or dotfile path in the sampled context.

low_findings.title: Screen Capture Alert Is Support Guidance
low_findings.description: The screen-related alert comes from support instructions about posting visible output and not posting screenshots of code. No evidence found of capture, upload, or exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled text discusses manual issue reporting and redaction. It does not instruct an agent or script to capture or upload the user screen.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Confirmation CLI Exchange Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The CCXT CLI is documented as executing requested exchange calls without asking for confirmation. Users should test with public endpoints or sandbox accounts before private trading actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states the no-confirmation behavior and broad method support. This is a real usage hazard, not malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local and Environment Credential Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Private CLI calls may read exchange credentials from keys.local.json and environment variables. This creates normal but sensitive handling of trading API secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited line directly names a local credentials file and environment variables. It is legitimate credential handling but needs user caution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy URL Prepending for API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The manual describes prepending proxy URLs to exchange API requests, including a public CORS proxy example. This can expose request metadata if users choose an untrusted proxy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly documented and can affect network routing. The risk depends on user configuration, so confidence is not higher.

content.value_statement: Crypto exchange APIs differ by venue and language. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use CCXT docs for market data, orders, and exchange support.
actual_capabilities: Explains CCXT installation and setup across JavaScript, Python, PHP, C#, Docker, and browser bundles.
Guides unified exchange API usage for market data, tickers, order books, trades, balances, and orders.
Summarizes CCXT Pro WebSocket concepts, watch methods, caching behavior, and streaming data limits.
Helps compare unified methods with exchange-specific implicit methods and exchange capability checks.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for verbose logs, timestamps, rate limits, proxies, and private API credentials.
Points users to CCXT reference files for CLI usage, supported exchanges, certification, and manual sections.
limitations: It is documentation support only and does not verify live exchange behavior or account permissions.
It cannot guarantee trading safety, profitability, regulatory compliance, or exchange availability.
Examples may need adaptation for each exchange, market type, symbol format, and API permission set.
Users must protect API keys and test private actions in sandbox or read-only modes when possible.

use_cases.title: Prototype Market Data Tools
use_cases.description: Find the right CCXT methods for tickers, order books, OHLCV data, trades, and exchange metadata.

use_cases.title: Debug Exchange Integration Issues
use_cases.description: Use troubleshooting guidance for verbose logs, timestamps, rate limits, symbol formats, and exchange-specific parameters.

use_cases.title: Plan Streaming Data Workflows
use_cases.description: Understand CCXT Pro watch methods, WebSocket subscriptions, cache windows, and REST fallback behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Understand a CCXT Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain which CCXT concepts I need for fetching ticker and order book data from one exchange.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Unified Methods
prompt_templates.prompt: Map my exchange integration goal to CCXT unified methods and note any exchange-specific checks I should make.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a Failed Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CCXT error description and tell me what verbose logs, credentials, symbols, or params I should verify.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Streaming Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CCXT Pro workflow for streaming orders and trades while managing cache limits, reconnects, and REST fallbacks.

output_examples.input: I need recent candles and the current order book for BTC trading analysis.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies OHLCV and order book methods, explains symbol and timeframe checks, and flags exchange-specific limits.

output_examples.input: My private order request fails with a timestamp or permission error.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends checking credentials, API permissions, clock drift, nonce behavior, rate limits, and redacted verbose logs.

output_examples.input: I want to use WebSockets for live trades and order updates.
output_examples.output: The skill explains watch methods, subscription lifecycle, cache windows, reconnection behavior, and when REST calls are still needed.

best_practices: Use unified methods first, then exchange-specific implicit methods only when a unified method is unavailable.
Keep API keys out of prompts, logs, screenshots, issue reports, and shared examples.
Test private trading workflows with sandbox, read-only keys, or small limits before live execution.
anti_patterns: Do not paste exchange API keys, secrets, or full private verbose logs into chat.
Do not run CLI private trading commands without confirming symbol, side, amount, price, and account mode.
Do not route authenticated exchange traffic through public proxies or untrusted CDN scripts.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants use CCXT documentation for exchange APIs, market data, orders, CLI usage, and WebSocket workflows.

faq.question: Does it place trades for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation guidance. Any trading code or CLI command must be reviewed and run by the user.

faq.question: Which languages does the documentation cover?
faq.answer: The referenced CCXT material covers JavaScript, Python, PHP, C#, Docker, browser bundles, and related async patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with CCXT Pro?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes references for WebSocket streaming, watch methods, cache behavior, and REST versus streaming differences.

faq.question: Is it safe to share verbose logs?
faq.answer: Share only redacted logs. Remove API keys, secrets, signatures, account identifiers, and private order details first.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but CCXT guidance can involve real funds, private credentials, CLI execution, proxies, and live exchange APIs.
